meta.title: Sound and Color
meta.subtitle: A Music Review of Tyler, the Creator's CHROMAKOPIA
meta.author: Armand Sotelo

body: Robert DiYanni opens his essay "Sound and Sense: Writing About Music" with a sobering confession: translating musical meaning into verbal language is an inherently imperfect enterprise. Music, he argues, is "a non-referential language with its own strictly musical meanings". One that resists neat articulation and refuses to be domesticated into prose. Yet the best music criticism, DiYanni contends, succeeds not by forcing music into words but by circling the sounds, inhabiting their emotional atmosphere, and making the ineffable at least approachable (DiYanni, n.d.). It is in this spirit that one must encounter Tyler, the Creator's eighth studio album, CHROMAKOPIA, a record that itself grapples with the challenge of self-expression, of translating a complex inner life into organized sound. More than any of his previous records, CHROMAKOPIA is a sustained meditation on what it means to be seen, to be misunderstood, to age in public, and to remain, against all odds, genuinely oneself. It is an album that rewards not just repeated listening but sustained critical attention, the kind that DiYanni associates with the most serious and rigorous engagement with music as an art form.

Released through Columbia Records on October 28, 2024, CHROMAKOPIA arrived with the momentum of an event. Dropped on a Monday morning at six o'clock with minimal advance notice, announced by shipping containers bearing its distinctive green cover art spotted in cities across the United States (Wikipedia, 2024). The album blends different genres including hip-hop, jazz, and soul, evoking Tyler's previous releases Flower Boy (2017) and Igor (2019), yet it represents something different from either of those projects: a record that abandons the alter-ego conceits of his past work in favor of something rawer and more personally immediate (Wikipedia, 2024). The title itself declares this interdisciplinary ambition. "Chromakopia" is a portmanteau of "chrome", a colored thing, and "kopia," the Polish word for copy, with the most prominent themes of the album being identity and self-discovery (Northern Light, 2024). Conceptually, the album is narrated by Tyler's mother, Bonita Smith, functioning like a diary that pieces together his perspective on past experiences separated by tracks (Wikipedia, 2024). Her voice: warm, firm, occasionally cracking with emotion, functions as both Greek chorus and moral compass, grounding the album's more abstract sonic flights in the irreducible fact of family, origin, and love.

sections.heading: Opening: "St. Chroma" and "Rah Tah Tah"
body: The album opens with "St. Chroma," and from its first seconds it establishes what kind of record this will be: enormous, unsettled, and demanding. The track begins with Bonita Smith's voice delivering a maternal manifesto — "You are the light / It's not on you, it's in you / Don't you ever in your motherfucking life dim your light for nobody" — before the sound of marching feet and chanting crowds swells beneath her words, creating a sense of ritual procession (Buseko, 2024). Turner describes this opening as "a magnificent start to such a long-awaited album," noting that the marching and chanting "creates the sense that destiny awaits" (Turner, 2024). The track is a meditation on artistic self-determination: Tyler insisting, against doubt and against the demands of fame, that he will make it out, that he will continue, that the light is not something imposed from outside but something innate and inextinguishable (Jaffin, 2024). Daniel Caesar's falsetto harmonics enter midway through, adding warmth and brightness to a track that might otherwise feel merely grandiose, and together they create one of the album's most sonically layered openings: the interplay of deep marching bass lines, soaring falsetto, and Tyler's own restrained verse creating what DiYanni might call a successful "mimetic stratagem," where sound and sense reinforce one another to produce a unified emotional impression (DiYanni, n.d.).

"Rah Tah Tah" follows immediately, and the tonal whiplash is deliberate. Where "St. Chroma" is expansive and ceremonial, "Rah Tah Tah" is compact and combative. A banger in the most classic sense of the word. Girvan describes it as "the first true-blue banger of the album, complete with a revving sub-bass, eerie synths, and piercing high-hats," noting that while the lyrics feature Tyler flexing about money and relationships, "given the sum of the core themes of the album established on the track prior, all of it feels like a mask, Tyler trying and failing to convince himself that material objects fulfill him" (Girvan, 2024). The track's braggadocio, Tyler declaring himself "the biggest out the city after Kenny," referring to Kendrick Lamar, is best understood not as simple boasting but as a kind of psychological armor, a performance of invulnerability that the rest of the album systematically dismantles (Wikipedia, 2024). Turner captures this dual quality astutely: "with all its boastfulness and badassery, 'Rah Tah Tah' feels like the old Tyler, and I think it's supposed to" (Turner, 2024). Musically, the pitched-up vocal samples and the relentless rhythmic drive of the percussion make the track feel almost manic, a deliberate contrast with the stateliness of "St. Chroma" that establishes one of the album's central structural logics: the oscillation between vulnerability and bravado, between the face and the mask.

sections.heading: Paranoia & Romance: "Noid" and "Darling, I"
body: Released as the album's lead single, "Noid" is CHROMAKOPIA's most structurally dissonant track and one of its most revealing. The title is the second half of the word "paranoid," and the track delivers precisely what that etymology promises: an unnerving sonic portrait of a man who cannot step outside without feeling surveilled, hunted, and consumed (Girvan, 2024). The backbone of the track is "a repeating pattern of drums, synthesizers, and electric guitars building up similar to the way it does in a horror movie", a deliberate deployment of musical suspense to externalize Tyler's psychological state (Jaffin, 2024). The song also incorporates a sample from the Zambian band Ngozi Family, sung in Nyanja, a Niger-Congo language. A moment of genuine interdisciplinary reach, where Tyler pulls African musical traditions into conversation with contemporary hip-hop anxiety (Duffy, 2024). DiYanni argues that the finest music criticism engages with the way music integrates multiple aesthetic traditions and meanings; "Noid" is precisely the kind of track that demands such interdisciplinary attention, because its meaning cannot be fully accessed without understanding its sonic architecture, its lyrical content, and the cultural references embedded in its production simultaneously. Tyler is not simply rapping about fame's discomforts; he is constructing an aural environment in which the listener is made to feel the paranoia themselves.

"Darling, I," which follows, performs an almost therapeutic function after the anxious intensity of "Noid." The track is one of the album's most melodically polished moments, built on piano chords and a grooving, mid-tempo beat that recalls the warmer textures of Igor while pushing into new harmonic territory (Girvan, 2024). Teezo Touchdown features on the track, his voice complementing Tyler's in a way that suggests shared understanding rather than mere collaboration. Green describes the song as Tyler's examination of "the fear of getting married, settling down and starting a family," and the track questions the concept of monogamy — Tyler rapping about how he seems to acquire everything from a single person, yet still finds himself searching even while standing at the altar (Green, 2024; Duffy, 2024). It is also, as DiYanni would appreciate, a track that successfully integrates the concerns of popular music aesthetics, the love song, the confession, the romantic lament, with the more classical aesthetic concerns of thematic development and structural coherence. The song does not simply express ambivalence; it enacts it, the musical groove pulling the listener toward emotional warmth even as the lyrics articulate withdrawal.

sections.heading: Adulthood and Costs: "Hey Jane," "Judge Judy," "I Killed You," and "Take Your Mask Off"
body: It is in the album's mid-section that CHROMAKOPIA reveals its most sophisticated narrative architecture. "Hey Jane" is one of Tyler's most structurally ambitious pieces of songwriting: an allegorical track that explores an unplanned pregnancy from both perspectives: Tyler's and the woman named Jane's. Using a back-and-forth written-letter structure that gives the track the formal quality of an epistolary novel set to music (Duffy, 2024). Chinapen describes how "the song does an excellent job at detailing both his and his significant other's points of view," praising its capacity to represent two radically different emotional realities within a single sonic space (Chinapen, 2024). Turner notes the song's resonant double meaning, as "Hey Jane" also functions as a reference to an online abortion pill, leaving the story's conclusion deliberately ambiguous. A moment of narrative restraint that is far more powerful than any explicit resolution would have been (Turner, 2024). The production here is deliberately spare: calm instrumentals guide the emotional journey without overwhelming it, creating a space where the lyrics carry the full weight of meaning. Sarapa aptly notes that "though it may not be played at a party, 'Hey Jane' crafts a beautiful and poetic song that charmingly tells a deep story" (Sarapa, 2024). This is DiYanni's vision of music writing realized in musical form: a song that does not merely describe emotional complexity but constructs it, making the listener inhabit a perspective they might otherwise never access.

"Judge Judy" moves from the anxious to the wistful, following Tyler as he falls for the eponymous Judy and then receives a letter explaining her sudden disappearance from his life. Duffy describes how Tyler accepts "the beauty of loss" in this track, a moment of emotional maturity unusual in hip-hop, where loss is more typically either raged against or aestheticized (Duffy, 2024). "I Killed You," which follows, shifts registers dramatically: a track built on an interpolation of "Wheels on the Bus" that frames the track's exploration of cultural identity and racial self-determination with an ironic, almost sinister tenderness (Han, 2024). The song features Childish Gambino contributing whimsical ad-libbing, and together they examine the practice of "killing off" parts of one's identity in order to survive the expectations and demands of a society that cannot accommodate the full complexity of a Black creative life (Duffy, 2024). It is a track that demands what DiYanni calls the integration of multiple interdisciplinary lenses, to fully understand "I Killed You," one must bring together musicological analysis, race theory, and an understanding of the particular pressures Tyler faces as a Black artist in a genre that has its own rigid codes of authenticity.

"Take Your Mask Off," featuring Daniel Caesar and Latoiya Williams, is the album's moral fulcrum. The track addresses a gay preacher hiding from his identity, a mother who feels her selfhood has been consumed by the demands of motherhood, and, implicitly, Tyler himself. Assembling a portrait of masking so comprehensive that it becomes universal (Duffy, 2024). Han describes the track as "a masterclass in sonic storytelling," noting how the instrumentation gradually strips away as the song progresses, "putting the emphasis on Tyler's voice and building up for a cathartic release at the end of the track" (Han, 2024). This dynamic range: the movement from dense, layered production to vulnerable simplicity, is one of the album's most powerful techniques, using musical form to mirror thematic content in a way that DiYanni would recognize as genuinely successful music writing: the organizational structure of the sound making sense of the emotional argument being advanced (DiYanni, n.d.). Chinapen's assessment that "from a songwriting perspective alone, 'Take Your Mask Off' offers some of Tyler's best bars on the album" feels accurate and insufficient simultaneously. This is a track where bars and production and emotional argument are so fully integrated that separating them falsifies what makes it great (Chinapen, 2024).

sections.heading: Commerce & Mortality: "Sticky," "Tomorrow," and "Thought I Was Dead"
body: "Sticky," featuring GloRilla, Sexyy Red, and Lil Wayne, is the album's most commercially-oriented track and also one of its most deliberate structural decisions. Coming immediately after the introspective vulnerability of "Take Your Mask Off," it arrives like a sudden burst of noise and light: heavy drumming layered with clapping and whistle chirps, each featured artist delivering their verse with the kind of exuberant confidence that "Take Your Mask Off" had just finished interrogating (Duffy, 2024). Duffy describes "Sticky" as one of "two of the best tracks on CHROMAKOPIA," noting how "relentless self-love is the major theme" and the track combines "humor with adamant confidence" (Duffy, 2024). Critics have been divided on whether this tonal swerve represents a crack in the album's coherence or a deliberate provocation. An argument that even an album as earnest and self-examining as CHROMAKOPIA can accommodate the pleasures of pure sound and spectacle. Attard, while conceding Tyler's brilliance at his best, argues that "Sticky" exemplifies moments where his tendency to layer in a brass section, truncated guest features, and an extended outro can overwhelm any sense of excitement (Attard, 2024). Whether one reads this as a flaw or a feature says something about what one values in an album of this scope and ambition.

"Tomorrow" arrives as one of the album's most quietly devastating moments. Built on a slow acoustic guitar progression and spare background vocals, the track addresses what Sarapa describes as "the existential fear of getting older" and "how immense pressure from others can lead to an awful cycle of comparisons and groundless self-hatred" (Sarapa, 2024). The lone guitar haunts the track with its melancholic chord progression, a deliberate choice to strip production to its barest elements at the moment when Tyler's emotional confessions are also at their most unguarded (Han, 2024). Bonita Smith opens the track by begging Tyler to give her a grandchild who looks just like him, a moment of familial pressure rendered so human and specific that it immediately grounds the song's otherwise abstract existential concerns (Duffy, 2024). Chinapen captures the track's emotional core precisely: Tyler voicing anxieties about growing old, his lyrics meditating on his mother's aging hands and graying hair, finding in these small physical details the weight of time's passage in a way that no grander artistic statement could achieve (Chinapen, 2024). This is DiYanni's "initial emotional impulse" transformed through craft into something that achieves genuine communicability, private grief becoming shared experience (DiYanni, n.d.).

"Thought I Was Dead," featuring ScHoolboy Q and Santigold, is arguably the album's greatest pure rap performance and one of Tyler's finest moments in any format. The track is built around insane trumpet work, a jazz-inflected brass arrangement that gives the song an almost martial energy, and Tyler's verse, which Herbers describes as "might be his best rapping ever," is a torrential display of technical skill and lyrical wit: quotable lines delivered with the ease of someone who has been saving them for exactly this moment (Herbers, 2024). Cox notes how ScHoolboy Q's contribution calls out the inauthentic, weird behavior of other rappers with "fun, fast-paced flows," while Tyler's own verse "points inward, referring to how he's 'changed like the fit got dirty'", yet promises to continue playing only by his own rules regardless (Cox, 2024). The track rejects imitation and cultural theft in favor of radical self-assertion, and the production reflects this: the counterpoint between the brass arrangement and the vocal performances creates the sense of two forces in dialogue, each interrupting and completing the other. This is DiYanni's vision of music writing made flesh in a song, "an awareness of the subjective nature of the music writing enterprise, both controlling and exploiting that subjectivity by a sureness of syntax and a penchant for creative analogy" (DiYanni, n.d.).

sections.heading: Gadamer's Fusion of Horizons: "Like Him," "Balloon," and "I Hope You Find Your Way Home"
body: Hans-Georg Gadamer's concept of the Fusion of Horizons — the idea that genuine understanding emerges from the dialogue between the interpreter's own horizon of experience and the horizon of the text or artwork being encountered — is the philosophical framework most useful for approaching CHROMAKOPIA's final movement. Tyler constructs a listening experience in which our pre-existing knowledge of his discography, his public persona, and our own personal histories are brought into active dialogue with the emotional landscape he is constructing. The album's penultimate stretch achieves this fusion most powerfully in "Like Him," which stands as CHROMAKOPIA's centerpiece and one of the most emotionally devastating songs Tyler has ever made. Buseko describes how "in 'Like Him,' Tyler engages in a profound conversation with his mother, addressing the complexities of growing up without a father figure," with Tyler's lyrics capturing his search for identity while simultaneously expressing forgiveness toward his mother for the choices she made, and her heartfelt admission: "It was my fault, not yours, not his", revealing her acknowledgment of past mistakes and her desire for Tyler to know that his father wanted to be present (Buseko, 2024). Wikipedia notes that critics praised the song for its emotional depth, introspective lyricism, and atmospheric production, with Lola Young's haunting vocal contribution highlighted as a particularly powerful element (Wikipedia — Like Him, 2025).

What makes "Like Him" so extraordinary in the context of the Fusion of Horizons is precisely its capacity to expand beyond Tyler's biography without abandoning its biographical specificity. Jackson observes that the song has touched the hearts of many who have grown up with emotionally or physically absent fathers, not because Tyler universalizes his experience, but because he particularizes it so precisely that listeners recognize their own particularities within it (Jackson, 2024). Gadamer would recognize this as the highest form of interpretive encounter: not the loss of the self in the text, but the enlargement of the self through the encounter. Andini, writing on Medium, captures the track's emotional arc with precision: the song begins with Bonita Smith telling a story about how Tyler resembles his absent father in both looks and behavior, and as Tyler grapples with this resemblance, chasing what he calls a ghost, his mother's voice at the end provides not resolution but something more honest: acknowledgment, vulnerability, and imperfect love (Andini, 2024). The production mirrors this emotional movement beautifully. The instrumentation begins spare and gradually expands, as if the act of speaking these truths aloud creates new sonic space for them to inhabit.

"Balloon," featuring Doechii, provides one of the album's few moments of genuine levity, and while some critics have found it the weakest entry on CHROMAKOPIA, it serves an important structural function: a breath of air before the album's closing statement (Matthews & Hartman, 2024). Duffy describes "Balloon" as another anthem combining "humor with adamant confidence," with Doechii's "incredibly rhythmic verses" making the track one of the album's most energetic feature collaborations (Duffy, 2024). The album then closes with "I Hope You Find Your Way Home". A quiet, almost hymn-like conclusion that circles back to the opening's questions about whether Tyler should keep the light on or bow out gracefully, and answers them not with a declaration but with a prayer. It is a closing that DiYanni might describe as achieving the full movement "from feeling to form and back again through form to a new and enriched kind of feeling" (DiYanni, n.d.), CHROMAKOPIA ending not with resolution but with the kind of open, resonant ambiguity that characterizes the best art.

sections.heading: Closing: Sound That Just Makes Sense
body: DiYanni's central argument in "Sound and Sense" is that the challenge of writing about music, of translating evanescent sound into communicable language, is also the challenge of music itself: to transform private feeling into public form, to make organized sound that can be heard, felt, and understood by someone other than its maker. CHROMAKOPIA meets this challenge with a comprehensiveness and emotional honesty that few albums of any era manage. Iowa State Daily puts it plainly: "This is Tyler's most definitive album. While on previous albums, you can feel him wearing his influences on his sleeve, here, it feels like his own thing completely. When an artist finally makes something completely themselves, it is a very satisfying thing to see and listen to" (Iowa State Daily, 2024). Rasul goes further, arguing that CHROMAKOPIA "stands as Tyler, the Creator's most cohesive artistic statement to date," one that "demands attention and rewards it generously" (Rasul, 2025). These assessments feel correct, even as they risk understating what makes the album significant: it is not merely that Tyler has made something entirely his own, but that "entire ownership" encompasses a range of human experience: paranoia, romantic ambivalence, racial identity, fatherhood, the fear of aging, the love of a mother. That few artists have the courage or craft to address simultaneously.

The album's musical terminology, its deployment of timbre, dynamics, counterpoint, groove, harmonic modulation, and rhythmic displacement, is always in service of emotional meaning rather than mere technical display. Lynch observes that Tyler's album removes his own mask and "unleashes his creativity by embracing his true self while encouraging his fans to do the same" (Lynch, 2024). Rasul captures this quality with particular clarity, arguing that the decision to have Bonita Smith narrate portions of the album "adds a familial dimension that grounds the more abstract concepts in real emotion," achieving "a level of vulnerability that feels earned rather than performed" (Rasul, 2025). This is the highest compliment one can pay to a record this ambitious: that its ambition feels not like overreach but like necessity, as if the album could not have been smaller than it is and still told the truth it needed to tell. CHROMAKOPIA is not an easy album. Its tonal whiplash, its genre-blending, its alternation between bravado and grief, can feel demanding on first listen and revealing on the fifth. But this is precisely what DiYanni means when he insists that good music writing, and, by extension, good music. "Makes sense in what it says directly" and "in what it suggests indirectly, in what circumscribes the music as well" (DiYanni, n.d.). CHROMAKOPIA is one of 2024's most significant artistic statements, and it will reward every future encounter with the generosity of an album that has not yet finished saying what it came to say.
